questions.question: Why does adding more clusters (increasing K) always reduce WCSS?

answers.b: Because each point gets closer to its assigned centroid

explanations.a: Increasing K reduces cluster size, not increases it.
explanations.b: More clusters mean centroids are closer to points, reducing squared distances.

questions.question: If a new feature that is highly correlated with an existing feature is added to a K-Means clustering dataset, what is the most likely outcome?

answers.c: The new feature may introduce redundancy without improving cluster quality

explanations.a: Adding more features does not guarantee better clustering performance.
explanations.b: Even if features are correlated, centroids do not become identical.
explanations.c: Highly correlated features often provide duplicate information, which may not enhance clustering.
explanations.d: WCSS becomes zero only when all data points coincide exactly with their centroids.

questions.question: Why does K-Means assume clusters are convex (spherical)?

answers.b: Because distance is measured from a single centroid

explanations.b: Distance-based assignment around centroids naturally forms spherical regions.

questions.question: What happens if K is chosen too small?

answers.b: Different groups may be merged into one cluster

explanations.a: This happens when K is too large.
explanations.b: Insufficient clusters force unrelated data into same cluster.
explanations.c: WCSS is not zero unless perfect fit.

questions.question: Why does K-Means require multiple runs with different initializations?

answers.b: To avoid poor local minima and improve solution quality

explanations.b: Different initializations help find better clustering.

questions.question: Why can K-Means fail when clusters have different densities and sizes simultaneously?

answers.a: Because it assumes uniform density and size

explanations.a: K-Means assumes similar density and size, causing misclassification.
explanations.b: Centroids are central to the algorithm.

questions.question: What does a Silhouette score close to 1 signify about the clustering result?

answers.b: Clusters are dense and well separated
answers.d: All clusters have equal number of samples

explanations.b: A score near 1 means points are much closer to their own cluster than to others.
explanations.c: Overlapping clusters result in scores near 0.
explanations.d: The metric does not measure cluster size equality.

questions.question: What is the main idea behind the Elbow Method in clustering?

answers.b: To identify the point where adding more clusters gives little improvement

explanations.a: WCSS keeps decreasing as K increases.
explanations.b: The elbow point represents diminishing returns in reducing WCSS.
explanations.c: Silhouette score is a separate method.

questions.question: What does a lower Davies–Bouldin score indicate?

answers.b: Better clustering with compact and distinct clusters

explanations.b: Lower values indicate better separation and compactness.
explanations.d: It does not directly indicate number of clusters.

questions.question: What does a low Silhouette score indicate?

answers.b: Points are near decision boundaries or misclassified

explanations.b: Low values indicate ambiguity in cluster assignment.
explanations.c: Perfect clustering corresponds to score near 1.
